item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although several tests have been scheduled and attempted to determine the function of SCP-3287-2, none of these were completed on account of the fact that all randomly-selected D-Class and animal test candidates were already sterile before the test began.

I find it very hard to swallow the idea that Foundation researchers could look at this and not think "Huh, maybe -2 retroactively sterilizes".

item.title: Re: If I'm right...
item.content:encoded: No, it completely erases any children. That's why the document had to be placed under a Scranton Reality Anchor, as the Hume stabilization was required to prevent the timeline from erasing the document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unintentional Johnny Cash reference there, talking about a Boy Named Sue?

EDIT: Clicked the link. Big oof.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a lot of stories with weapons that retroactively erase someone from history. This is the first one I've seen in a long time that accurately portrays (part of) what it would be like to own that weapon. "We know what we built it for, but we don't know if it works, and we can't figure out why…"

The twist at the end is novel, and might be considered arbitrarily worse if not for the fact that it calls attention to the real horror of omega-K. The Foundation was planning for a world where everything would have to be sterilized, not just individual humans, and a RetGun won't suffice when you need to target insects by the million. And once you've successfully aerosolized the stuff, you've got to be a lot more careful with it…

It makes you wonder how often this happens with the ordinary antipersonnel sort of weapon. If not for the SRA, there wouldn't be any evidence of the testing left behind. So if a particularly lousy customer asked someone to build them a RetGun, and then turned around and shot the engineer with it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They'd both erase the child, yes. -3 would also erase the parent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Spoiler
- Hide Spoiler

while I like the ending and ending line a lot I think I missed what makes it special.

The -3 vial retroactively sterilizes your parents. Dr. Kaito Shoga Jr. locked the recipe under a Scranton Reality Anchor so it wouldn't be erased from history after he accidentally exposed himself to 3287-3.

Director Kaito Shoga (Sr.) was born sterile and never had a son.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was this stuff made using powdered unicorn horn, because in an MC&D article it shows that powdered unicorn horn is a retrocausal agent which erases the person ever having sex, including children, giving them back virginity. It seems like if you mixed it with a bunch of other substances you could enhance it so it renders the the subject retrocausally sterile rather than a virgin

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit that I initially had a negative impression of this due to the PM BS, but after a reread, I'll say that I actually like this article a fair ammount. What really hits home for me is the Message left on 3287-0. I always have a liking for skips that have something like "yeah we screwed up" attached to this

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Slides off of me. The brevity is nice, but the skip itself feels so inconsequential for what it can do. It felt pretty boring, so I can't say I got much out of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whooooooooppppsss sorry!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But doesn't this mean that -2 is functionally identical to -3, if you gave -2 to a parent of an existing child? So it would have the same effect of erasing that child from existence?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are thousands of failed timelines where the earth becomes home for immortal bugs!

(Also you commented this on the skip, not the tale, silly Cone)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My headcanon for Wilson is that he sterilised a whole bunch of bugs, offsetting the bugpocalypse by another couple of weeks or so. And then in each subsequent timeline, Mesmur talks to Wilson a little bit later than she did before, and this whole tale keeps happening over and over forever in a thousand different timelines. And the end result is that nobody actually did anything, but there are hardly any bugs.

Which brings me to another point: assuming using 3287-3 creates a new timeline, what happens in the original timeline?

item.title: Admin Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
I guess I gotta make it clearer.

Staff know about the PMs. Please do not post about the PMs anymore in this thread. If you receive similar PMs from other trolls, do not respond to them, or post about them in whichever discussion thread of an article they link to.

Do not post any further about the PMs in this article, or Scantron's article, or any future cases like this.

This is a closed staff post. Do not reply to this post for any reason if you are not staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was messaged too, using a throwaway account. I think another person is trying to make it seem like someone's cheating. As for the article? I liked it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly I want to second this. Those three paragraphs need to be rewritten.

Some of my issues I've had reading this article that made me not upvote are:

  • If they're indistinguishable except for their effects, and we really don't know what two and three really do (arguably, three's the only one that we definitely don't know what it does) how do we even know there's three different types, and not different reactions to the same type? It might be better explained as 'there's a small chance of this happening in every ██.█% of test subjects,' but that's just one suggestion of how you could handle that.
  • The numbering should really start at SCP-3287-1 for the instruction manual and 2-4 for the compounds made, because the instructions are part of the SCP, but that's nitpicky.
  • The unicorn horn is way to similar to this SCP without making changes as to how this SCP works. You do have the advantage that the unicorn horn is a natural resource rather than a synthetic once, but it would be easiest to make a distinction between the effects of the unicorn horn and the effects of the gas when you go to edit the paragraphs for the first bullet.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
'image sourced from wikidot' is not a sensical phrase.

You mean, image sourced from Pixabay.

https://pixabay.com/en/gas-weld-welder-protective-gas-2147493/

(CC0 Creative Commons; Free for commercial use; No attribution required)

(but it's still good to track provenance)

item.title: Moderator Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
Staff is aware. Suffice to say, the message spammer is not the author.

This is a closed staff post. Please do not reply unless you are staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was asked to review the SCP, and while I think it's decent. I also think it should be reworded. Maybe for example, using the words 'form, variant etc' instead of type to describe the three varieties of this SCP? NineofSwords comment pretty much catches all the little and major things that I noticed in the page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Think that might be somebody else's sock my man.

item.title: You are not alone
item.content:encoded: Looks that you are not the only one who had this message

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
risenxenomorph666
To: DrConnors
Opinion? 8 Jun 2018, 08:28
Tell me if you like this: http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-3287

Thanks.

Good article.

Spamming my inbox with it? I WISH I COULD DOWNVOTE IT 500 TIMES, just on principle.

So I'm gonna stick with a novote.

EDIT- It obviously isn't the author/team, so I'll change to an upvote.

Spammers need to be IPbanned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's also an issue that the Foundation should have some Horn on tap, given that MCD notes that the Foundation has samples.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Review below

+ show block
– hide block

[All types of SCP-3287 are nearly identical in chemical composition, but are distinguished by unique anomalous compounds that supposedly alter their effects.]
This sentence is contradicting itself. "Nearly identical in chemical composition" means that the individual molecules in can 1 are the all the same, but maybe have an extra carbon or a nitrogen instead of a carbon compared to the molecules in can 2, while "distinguished by unique anomalous compounds" means that most of the molecules in can 1 and can 2 are the same but there's a little bit of different anomalous molecules added in each. Basically the first means the perfume bottles are ethyl alcohol and isopropyl alcohol respectively, while the second means that they're both ethyl alcohol but the first has a little bit of lemon and the second has orange. I think you're intending the first one, to rank them under SCP-3287 and [Despite being similar in composition to SCP-3287-1]. To do that you could say something like [Differences in chemical composition alter their anomalous effects.], sharpened up by your writing.

[This has been used to artificially preserve certain species of animals in the wild by sterilizing their predators]
…and driving their predators to extinction instead. Maybe better to say that we used them to sterilize human predators, or preserve species that make valuable products like that amnestic-making cactus, instead of something that's going to fuck with the ecosystem whichever way you slice it.

[and also the type with the most documented information]
Passive voice sounds weird and leads to wait, what? moments like this. Try [the type that is the most researched] instead of [most documented information], Foundation is the one doing the documenting of the information. It's still passive voice but more clear.

[SCP-3287-3 is the third type of SCP-3287. There is no documentation of any attempted tests with SCP-3287-3.]
Except maybe that's what you intended, for the antimemetic/Foundation to be unsure if they've actually done that research or not. It's still kind of clumsy, given that Foundation is also referring to the fact that they have done more logged tests with SCP-3287-1 than logged tests with SCP-3287-2 rather than just they've done a lot of tests with SCP-3287-1 and some number that is eating itself with SCP-3287-3.

[An endeavour to only select test candidates who are not sterile has not been attempted.]
Haha, I can imagine a professor shaking their head at those darn interns picking out steriles to defertilize.

I like the idea of memory removal (forgetting that the kids exist? removing the kids from existence?) -3, - was a nice lead up to it. The length of this makes it short and sweet. I had deja vu with -2 to unicorn horns, but that might be a nice thing to have -2 be unicorn horns (retroactive sterilization and death of offspring) with Foundation finding it as convergent evolution, to lead up to -3 causing offspring to be forgotten and uh something specific and awful with ΩK. I'm still not really sure what it does that differs it from -2, and while I like the ending and ending line a lot I think I missed what makes it special.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: opps my comment was transferred from another thread or something. Anyway good article, pretty short but leaves just enough for the readers imagination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The weird thing is that there seems to be nothing inherently anomalous about -1, and thus the connection to -2 and -3 isn't apparent.

SCP-3287-1 is the first type of SCP-3287

Also, this is really bad writing, and I recommend tightening up all three of these paragraphs.

Still, upvote because the effects of -2 and -3 are laid well without spelling them out.

item.title: If I'm right...
item.content:encoded:
So, if I'm reading this right, then 2 makes it so that the one it's used on was always sterile, and 3 makes it so that one of the subject's parents had always been sterile, and erases some, but not all, things that are only true because the affected person had not initially been sterile?

Or is it that it alters the timeline completely, but the Scranton Reality Anchor keeps the one guy's research from being undone, so removing the note from the anchor's field of effect would turn it blank or make it vanish?

Also, as much as I enjoyed the article, I think it's odd that even though I could figure it out this quickly, the Foundation hasn't. They're just kind of uncharacteristically dense here; I think the article would be improved if you accounted for this somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looks more reasonable to me, but you typo'd as Kaita near the bottom of the page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tried my best to differentiate it from the Powdered Unicorn Horn, because I know the ideas are definitely similar, I'm sorry that it still felt too similar in the end for you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed the name up so it is (hopefully) more legitimate

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While SCP-3287-3 is supposed to be the punch of the article, it feels far too similar to a specific mythical creature's horn for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To the best of my (again, minimal) knowledge, naming a son Kaede would be like naming your son Sue. I think it'd be confusing at the very least, though my recommendation would be to wait to see if someone more knowledgeable than I am to confirm/refute.

As for the last name, Google is telling me that ginger translates to 生姜 which is read as Shoga, and I'm seeing no mention of Shokyo at all. I'm having trouble finding any mention of Shokyo as any kind of word, let alone a name. Again, I'd wait for someone smarter, but I thought it might be worth bringing it up for you at the very least, so I'm sorry if it sounds really nitpicky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read on a different site that Kaede was gender neutral, and that Shokyo meant "Ginger"

The significance of the name isn't really major, it just sounded like an interesting name to me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll be the first to admit that my knowledge of Japanese isn't the best, but I figured it wouldn't hurt to ask:

Was the researcher's name picked for any particular reason? As far as I can tell, Shokyo isn't a genuine surname in Japanese, showing zero results on jisho.org. Likewise, Kaede is an exclusively feminine name according to the same website.

It's not necessarily immersion breaking, but with my minimal knowledge it comes across as gibberish and I'm wondering if that's because I'm missing something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*Temporal shenanigans intensifies*

+1 for the Foundation and it's pop-control project.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
And now, I get to contribute to the Doomsday Contest!

Thanks to my team, Captain Kirby, Croquembouche and Veiedhimaedhr for the help, as well as Rimple and Taffeta for extra outside crit!

I hope you all enjoy!

Image source

Edit: Included proper link to image (Thank you Voct)
